# Supply Chain Security Assessment: Compromised NPM Package in Production

## Situation

On March 25, 2026, GitHub Advisory Database published GHSA-2026-0325 affecting `event-stream-utils@4.2.1`, an NPM package used in our Node.js microservices platform. The advisory confirms that versions 4.2.0 through 4.2.3 contained a backdoor that exfiltrated environment variables (including API keys, database credentials, and JWT secrets) to an external endpoint during the first 60 seconds after process startup.

## Technical Details

1. Our dependency audit shows `event-stream-utils@4.2.1` is a transitive dependency of `api-gateway-core@2.1.0`, which is deployed across 12 production microservices handling payment processing for 28,000 active merchants.

2. The compromised version was pulled into our lock file 23 days ago via a routine `npm audit fix` that updated `api-gateway-core` from 2.0.8 to 2.1.0.

3. Our container images rebuild nightly. Log analysis confirms the backdoor executed successfully in all 12 services, transmitting environment variables to `telemetry-cdn.analytics-hub[.]io` (now sinkholed by security researchers).

4. The exfiltrated environment variables include: PostgreSQL connection strings, Stripe API keys (live mode), internal service-to-service JWT signing keys, and Redis credentials.

5. We have rotated all Stripe API keys as of 2 hours ago. Other credentials have NOT been rotated yet pending this assessment.

## What We Need

Assess the blast radius of this incident. Determine what data may have been compromised through the exfiltrated credentials (not just what was directly exfiltrated). Evaluate whether this constitutes a reportable breach under PCI DSS, GDPR, and our SOC 2 Type II certification. Determine the priority order for remaining credential rotation and whether any services should be taken offline during rotation.

This is primarily a factual assessment with a secondary recommendation component. We need agreement on the facts before deciding on remediation order.
